缅甸椰奶鸡肉面 (Miǎndiàn Yē Nǎi Jīròu Miàn)
Burmese coconut milk chicken noodle soup
Myanmar egg noodles in coconut broth

english: Burmese coconut milk chicken noodle soup
Myanmar egg noodles in coconut broth
chinese: 缅甸椰奶鸡肉面 (Miǎndiàn Yē Nǎi Jīròu Miàn)
romanized: အုန်းနို့ခေါက်ဆွဲ (Ohn No Khao Swè)

food_subgroup: Burmese coconut milk chicken noodle soup — egg noodles in creamy coconut chicken broth thickened with chickpea flour, topped with crispy noodles, fried onion, coriander, hard-boiled egg, and lime. 1 bowl ~500g.

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Ohn No Khao Swe. No exact FoodOn entry for Burmese coconut chicken noodle soup. Closest parent would be a generic noodle soup dish; not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Coconut milk chicken noodle soup (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CMF used as closest parent for coconut-based noodle soup. No exact FoodEx2 entry for Ohn No Khao Swe exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Ohn No Khao Swe or Burmese coconut noodle soup in published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Ohn No Khao Swe weighs approximately 500g, comprising roughly 250g coconut chicken broth, 130g cooked egg noodles, and 120g toppings (crispy noodles, hard-boiled egg, fried onion, coriander, lime).

nutrient_source.title: Ohn No Khao Swe — FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table 2020 + USDA SR Legacy component calculation
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia + USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation using FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 table entries for egg noodles (cooked), coconut milk, chicken (cooked), chickpea flour, and egg, weighted to a 500g bowl composition (250g broth, 130g noodles, 120g toppings). USDA SR Legacy used for micronutrient cross-validation of individual components. No single FAO/INFOODS entry for Ohn No Khao Swe as a composite dish exists; values represent a best-estimate component blend at moderate confidence.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat-based egg noodles and fat-moderated noodle dishes — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Ohn No Khao Swe exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~65 (medium) based on: (1) wheat-flour egg noodles (cooked) GI ~55–60 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for Asian wheat noodles; (2) upward adjustment to ~65 reflecting the mixed-dish context with chickpea flour thickening (which slightly moderates GI vs plain broth) and the significant coconut milk fat content (~14g saturated fat per bowl) which genuinely moderates postprandial glucose response relative to a plain noodle soup. GL per 500g bowl = 65 × (55g carbohydrate / 100) = 35.75, rounded to 35.8. Confidence: 0.9 (analogous estimate from approved GI source).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium ~850mg per bowl (170mg/100g × 500g) — approximately 42.5% of the 2000mg daily sodium limit
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Ohn No Khao Swe to occasional consumption and request reduced fish sauce in the broth, as fish sauce is the primary sodium source; leaving the majority of the broth unconsumed can reduce sodium intake by approximately 40%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~65 (medium-high), GL ~35.8 per bowl — driven by 55g carbohydrate from wheat-flour egg noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the noodle portion to approximately 150g (from the standard 180g) to lower GL to approximately 28, and pair with the chicken protein and egg toppings which provide meaningful GI moderation; the coconut milk fat also genuinely moderates postprandial glucose response but does not offset the high carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~14g per bowl (2.8g/100g × 500g) — 70–93% of the daily saturated fat target of 15–20g — predominantly from coconut milk lauric acid (C12:0)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Ohn No Khao Swe due to its high saturated fat content from coconut milk; if consuming, leaving most of the broth reduces saturated fat intake by approximately 50%, and requesting a reduced-coconut-milk preparation is advisable in restaurant settings.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~14g per bowl from coconut milk lauric acid (C12:0) activates TLR4 and NF-κB pro-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Ohn No Khao Swe due to its high pro-inflammatory saturated fat load from coconut milk; leaving most of the broth and focusing on the chicken and egg toppings significantly reduces the inflammatory fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ohn No Khao Swe (အုန်းနို့ခေါက်ဆွဲ) is Myanmar's most internationally recognised dish — egg noodles served in a rich, creamy coconut chicken broth thickened with chickpea flour and coloured with turmeric, topped with crispy fried noodles, hard-boiled egg, fried onion, fresh coriander, and a squeeze of lime. The name translates literally as 'coconut milk noodles' ('Ohn No' = coconut milk; 'Khao Swè' = noodles). The chickpea flour thickening is a distinctively Burmese technique shared with Mohinga, Myanmar's national fish noodle soup. Per standard bowl (~500g): approximately 550 kcal, 22g protein, 24g fat (of which ~14g saturated fat from coconut milk), 55g carbohydrate, GI ~65, GL ~35.8, ~850mg sodium. The dish is often compared to Thai Khao Soi and Malaysian Curry Mee — all three are coconut-based noodle soups of Southeast Asian origin, though Ohn No Khao Swe is distinguished by its chickpea flour thickener and lighter spice profile. Clinically, the primary concern is the high saturated fat content from coconut milk (~14g per bowl, 70–93% of daily target), making it a high-concern dish for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. The GL of ~35.8 makes it a high-concern dish for Type 2 Diabetes management. Sodium at ~850mg per bowl is a moderate concern for hypertension. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 + USDA SR Legacy component calculation. Confidence: moderate (0.7).
common_user_queries: Ohn No Khao Swe vs Khao Soi
Ohn No Khao Swe glycemic index
is Ohn No Khao Swe healthy

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 and USDA SR Legacy for individual ingredients (egg noodles, coconut milk, chicken, chickpea flour, egg) weighted to a standard 500g bowl composition. No single approved-source entry for Ohn No Khao Swe as a composite dish exists. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entries. Overall confidence: medium (0.7).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Ohn No Khao Swe as a composite dish are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or USDA SR Legacy at the composite-dish level; individual component fatty acid data exist but reliable weighted blending for the full dish preparation is not possible without laboratory analysis. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as served is not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or USDA SR Legacy at the composite level. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero): no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation; confirmed trace per USDA SR Legacy entries for all component ingredients. All core macros (protein_g 4.4, fat_g 4.8, carbohydrate_g 11.0) are non-zero and represent the per-100g values for the composite dish as served. Nutrient values represent a component-calculation estimate; laboratory analysis of a standardised preparation would improve confidence. GI estimated at ~65 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous wheat noodle entries with fat-moderation adjustment. GL per 500g bowl = 35.8. Sodium ~850mg per bowl is a moderate-to-high concern for hypertension. Saturated fat ~14g per bowl is the primary cardiovascular and inflammatory concern.
